1: YHWH commands Moses to give Israel the precise borders of the land before entry. The land is not undefined space — it is a named, bounded inheritance (nachalah) with specific geography. God had promised Abraham 'this land' (Genesis 15:18); now he delivers coordinates.
2: 'Command the Israelites and say: when you enter the land of Canaan, this is the land that shall fall to you as inheritance (nachalah), the land of Canaan according to its borders (gevuloteha).' Gevul (border, boundary) frames all that follows — the gift has edges, which means the gift is real and specific, not merely symbolic.
3: The southern boundary begins at the wilderness of Zin along the border of Edom. The south is defined by known terrain — Zin (the desert where Miriam died, Numbers 20) and Edom (the nation that refused passage, Numbers 20:14-21). Even hostile terrain becomes a boundary marker for God's gift.
4: The border turns from the south of the Ascent of Akrabbim, passes through Zin, and its limit shall be south of Kadesh-barnea. Kadesh — where the spies reported and the people rebelled (Numbers 13-14) — now marks a corner of the inheritance. The place of failure becomes a border post of the promised land.
5: From Kadesh-barnea it goes to Hazar-addar and continues to Azmon. The specificity is remarkable — these are not approximations but demarcated points, suggesting a real administrative boundary Israel was expected to recognize and maintain.
6: The western boundary (yam, sea) is simply 'the Great Sea' — the Mediterranean. The sea itself is the boundary, natural and absolute. Israel is not a seafaring nation in the Mosaic vision; the horizon of the sea marks the western limit of the inheritance.
7: The northern boundary: from the Great Sea to Mount Hor. This Hor is different from the Hor in Edom where Aaron died (Numbers 20:22) — geographical terminology reused for a different location, a reminder that names recur in Scripture without always denoting the same place.
8: From Mount Hor to Lebo-hamath. Lebo-hamath ('entrance of Hamath') marks the northern gateway into Aramean territory. This boundary anticipates the later Davidic empire's extent; it is the promise in geographic form.
9: The boundary ends at Zedad, then continues to Ziphron, and its limit shall be Hazar-enan. These northern points anchor the promise in real geography, known to ancient Near Eastern cartographers though obscure to modern readers.
10: The eastern boundary from Hazar-enan to Shepham. The east side of the promised land runs through territory contested between Aram and Israel in later centuries. The boundary is not merely legal — it is eschatological, projecting what Israel's inheritance should ultimately encompass.
11: From Shepham the border descends to Riblah on the east side of Ain, then down to the shoulder of the Sea of Chinnereth (the Sea of Galilee). Riblah reappears in Jeremiah as the site of Judah's judgment (Jeremiah 39:5-6) — the same points that mark the promised inheritance later mark exile.
12: The border continues down the Jordan and ends at the Salt Sea (Dead Sea). 'This is the land with its borders all around.' The Jordan, from Galilee to the Dead Sea, forms the eastern boundary. The river that Israel crossed miraculously (Josh 3) becomes the permanent eastern marker of the inheritance.
13: Moses commands: 'This is the land you shall apportion by lot among yourselves, which YHWH has commanded to give to nine and a half tribes.' Goral (lot) signals divine sovereignty — the size of each tribe's portion is ultimately God's determination, not human negotiation. Lot-casting in the Ancient Near East was understood as divine decision.
14: Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received their inheritance east of the Jordan already (Numbers 32). Their decision to settle Transjordan, born of pragmatism (good pastureland), means they receive outside the boundaries just described. The Transjordanian tribes exist in a kind of borderland status throughout Israel's history.
15: Two and a half tribes received east of the Jordan at Jericho, toward the sunrise. 'Toward the sunrise' (mizrach hashemesh) — the east, the direction of dawn, frames the Transjordanian settlement not as inferior but as oriented toward light. Yet the full promise of Canaan remained west of the Jordan.
16: YHWH speaks: 'These are the names of the men who shall apportion the land among you.' The process of inheritance distribution is not improvised — YHWH designates specific leaders. The land-gift comes with administrative structure, not chaos.
17: Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun will lead the apportionment. The pairing is significant: priestly authority (Eleazar) and military-civil authority (Joshua) together manage the inheritance. Neither alone — sacred and civil together serve the distribution of God's gift.
18: One leader from each tribe shall be appointed to apportion the land. The process is tribal, representative, and inclusive. Every tribe has a voice in receiving what God promised their ancestor.
19: From the tribe of Judah: Caleb son of Jephunneh. Caleb survives from the spy generation (Numbers 13-14) specifically because he 'followed YHWH wholeheartedly' (Numbers 14:24). His presence here as Judah's representative is a quiet vindication — the faithful outlast the unfaithful.
20: From Simeon: Shemuel son of Ammihud. Simeon's tribe would later be absorbed largely into Judah (Joshua 19:1), receiving no distinct territory. Yet here they stand equally in the apportionment process — the future diminishment is not yet written.
21: From Benjamin: Elidad son of Kislon. Benjamin's territory would later contain Jerusalem (on its border with Judah) and Saul's hometown, Gibeah. The small tribe with large significance.
22: From the tribe of Dan: Bukki son of Jogli. Dan's territory would prove difficult to hold — the tribe would eventually migrate north (Judg 18), partly explaining why Dan is absent from the tribal listing in Revelation 7.
23: From Manasseh (the half-tribe west of Jordan): Hanniel son of Ephod. The full tribe of Manasseh is split — half east (already settled, v14-15) and half west (still to be apportioned). Hanniel represents the western half.
24: From Ephraim: Kemuel son of Shiphtan. Ephraim — the younger son whom Jacob blessed ahead of Manasseh (Gen 48) — receives the larger territory in the central highlands and gives the region its central significance.
25: From Zebulun: Elizaphan son of Parnach. Zebulun's territory in the Galilee region would become notable in Isaiah's 'land of Zebulun' prophecy (Isaiah 9:1) and its fulfillment in Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matthew 4:13-16).
26: From Issachar: Paltiel son of Azzan. Issachar settles in the Jezreel Valley — fertile agricultural land. The tribe is later characterized as 'understanding the times' (1 Chronicles 12:32), suggesting wisdom alongside material blessing.
27: From Asher: Ahihud son of Shelomi. Asher's coastal territory in the northern Galilee would be associated with maritime trade. The prophetess Anna (Luke 2:36) was from Asher — the tribe persisting to the New Testament era.
28: From Naphtali: Pedahel son of Ammihud. Naphtali's territory alongside the Sea of Galilee becomes the heartland of Jesus's early ministry (Matthew 4:15). The 'land of Naphtali' that Isaiah promised light to (Isaiah 9:1) receives its fulfillment in the one who is the light of the world.
29: These are the men whom YHWH commanded to apportion the inheritance to Israel in the land of Canaan. The chapter closes with the full list named. The inheritance is not an abstraction — it has named recipients, named boundaries, and named administrators. The promise is taking concrete form at the threshold of its realization.
